title: Structural modeling of 2019-novel coronavirus (nCoV) spike protein reveals a proteolytically-sensitive activation loop as a distinguishing feature compared to SARS-CoV and related SARS-like coronaviruses

match.title: Structural modeling of 2019-novel coronavirus (nCoV) spike protein reveals a proteolytically-sensitive activation loop as a distinguishing feature compared to SARS-CoV and related SARS-like coronaviruses
match.authorString: Jaimes JA, André NM, Millet JK, Whittaker GR.

authorAffiliation.affiliation: Department of Microbiology and Immunology, College of Veterinary Medicine, Cornell University, Ithaca NY 14853 USA
Department of Microbiology and Immunology, College of Veterinary Medicine, Cornell University, Ithaca NY 14853 USA
Virologie et Immunologie Moléculaires, INRAE, Université Paris-Saclay, 78352 Jouy-en-Josas, France
Department of Microbiology and Immunology, College of Veterinary Medicine, Cornell University, Ithaca NY 14853 USA

match.abstractText: The 2019 novel coronavirus (2019-nCoV) is currently causing a widespread outbreak centered on Hubei province, China and is a major public health concern. Taxonomically 2019-nCoV is closely related to SARS-CoV and SARS-related bat coronaviruses, and it appears to share a common receptor with SARS-CoV (ACE-2). Here, we perform structural modeling of the 2019-nCoV spike glycoprotein. Our data provide support for the similar receptor utilization between 2019-nCoV and SARS-CoV, despite a relatively low amino acid similarity in the receptor binding module. Compared to SARS-CoV, we identify an extended structural loop containing basic amino acids at the interface of the receptor binding (S1) and fusion (S2) domains, which we predict to be proteolytically-sensitive. We suggest this loop confers fusion activation and entry properties more in line with MERS-CoV and other coronaviruses, and that the presence of this structural loop in 2019-nCoV may affect virus stability and transmission.

SciScore is an automated tool that is designed to assist expert reviewers by finding and presenting formulaic information scattered throughout a paper in a standard, easy to digest format. SciScore checks for the presence and correctness of RRIDs (research resource identifiers), and for rigor criteria such as sex and investigator blinding. About SciScore

SciScore is an automated tool that is designed to assist expert reviewers by finding and presenting formulaic information scattered throughout a paper in a standard, easy to digest format. SciScore is not a substitute for expert review. SciScore checks for the presence and correctness of RRIDs (research resource identifiers) in the manuscript, and detects sentences that appear to be missing RRIDs. SciScore also checks to make sure that rigor criteria are addressed by authors. It does this by detecting sentences that discuss criteria such as blinding or power analysis. SciScore does not guarantee that the rigor criteria that it detects are appropriate for the particular study. Instead it assists authors, editors, and reviewers by drawing attention to sections of the manuscript that contain or should contain various rigor criteria and key resources. narrative:
Short impact summary

The study of the 2019-novel coronavirus (nCoV) spike protein by Jaimes et al. has generated significant scholarly interest, reflected in its 511 citations on OpenAlex. The distinctive findings related to a proteolytically-sensitive activation loop position this research as an influential precursor to formal publication in a reputable journal.

Full narrative

Jaimes and colleagues' preprint on the structural modeling of the nCoV spike protein stands out due to its immediate relevance and scientific innovation, providing insights that differentiate it from the SARS-CoV family. This work has been recognized with 511 citations, showcasing its substantial impact on the field of virology and structural biology since its sharing on bioRxiv.

The preprint, which eventually transitioned to a peer-reviewed publication in a prominent journal, is a testament to the authors' commitment to open science practices. Engaging the scientific community early, this research drew attention before its formal publication, where it continued to attract interest and discussion, further evidenced by 505 citations in its published version.

Institutionally, the authors are affiliated with renowned entities such as Cornell University and Université Paris-Saclay, enhancing the credibility and collaborative ethos behind the research. This backing, combined with the geographical footprint illustrated by citations from countries including the United States, United Kingdom, and Brazil, underscores the international significance of their findings.

By identifying a unique activation loop in the spike protein, the study stands as a critical piece of foundational knowledge that has enabled subsequent research, demonstrating a clear trajectory of proven downstream reuse with a combined reuse count of 512. The work not only adds to the understanding of coronavirus biology but also contributes to the ongoing efforts in viral outbreak preparedness and response.
